Raw egg white is 51% digestible. Cooked, it's 91%. When you cook an egg you nearly DOUBLE the protein the body can actually use — Evenepoel et al. measured it with stable isotopes.

The mistake: thinking heat only changes texture and flavour. It doesn't. It unlocks nutrition that raw food physically withholds. Every sear, every braise, you're not seasoning a plate — you're making the food usable.

The full chemistry, and why it built the human brain, is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: A startling before/after number stated flat as line one, with the named study for evidence and a clean curiosity gap on the mechanism.

posts.subhook: A big brain or a big gut
posts.caption:
Every other primate chose the gut. We chose the brain. There was no third option.

Your brain is 2% of your body and burns 20-25% of your energy at rest — 86 billion neurons that no chimp can afford. The reason we can run that bill while a chimp can't comes down to one thing they do for six hours a day and we do for less than one.

The metabolic trade that made you a person — and the surplus it freed — is in the article. Link in bio.
posts.why: Contrarian framing (forced trade-off) plus a vivid stat grid, withholding the chewing-time payoff that resolves it — pure curiosity gap.

posts.caption:
A chimp chews for six hours a day. You eat in under one. That gap is where language, tool-making, teaching and storytelling came from — every cultural achievement you can name was built on time that cooking handed back.

Cooking is external digestion: heat ruptures cell walls and denatures protein before food enters the body, so you net 30-50% more calories from the same raw ingredients. Less chewing, more calories, free time. That's the whole human project in one trade.

The evidence chain, from Wonderwerk Cave to your galley, is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Two concrete numbers up front (6 hours, 30-50%) and a sweeping payoff, but the how (external digestion mechanism) is the reason to click through.

posts.subhook: The cultures that eat only raw
posts.caption:
Across the entire anthropological record, the number of human societies that eat only raw food is zero. Not one. Cooking isn't a preference — it's a species-level requirement, and the hearth is older than the wall.

Controlled fire dates to roughly one million years ago at Wonderwerk Cave: ash and charred bone deep inside, where lightning can't reach. The campfire was the first classroom too — daytime talk was 6% storytelling; around the night fire it jumped to 81%.

A million years of unbroken practice, and why you're still tending that fire — in the article. Link in bio.
posts.why: A flat, absolute number (zero) as a myth-bust, backed by two striking secondary stats, opening a gap on the million-year lineage.

posts.caption:
Above 140°C, amino acids and sugars collide and throw off more than 600 distinct flavour compounds — the crust on bread, the sear on a steak, the roast in coffee. That hiss when protein hits the pan IS the Maillard reaction starting.

Most cooks run this chemistry every day and couldn't name a single step. Below the threshold you get grey and steamed; above it, brown and complex. The line is exact, and so is what it costs you to miss it.

The full breakdown — Maillard, caramelisation, why cooked tomatoes give 5x the lycopene — is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: A precise temperature and a specific count (140°C, 600+ compounds) tied to a sensory detail every chef knows (the hiss), with the threshold-mistake as personal stakes.

galley-efficiency-protocol.title: Galley Efficiency Protocol: Maximizing Output in Minimal Space

posts.caption:
If any ingredient takes more than 60 seconds to grab from where you stand, your station is built wrong.

Here's the audit: stand at your primary cooking position and time yourself retrieving every component of your most complex dish. Anything over a minute gets moved closer or pre-staged before the next service. Most chefs have never timed it once, and it's quietly costing them the whole plate.

The full retrieval protocol is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Specific number (60s) + a self-test that exposes a flaw they can't see, opening a curiosity gap on the audit method.

posts.caption:
A yacht galley gives you 4 to 12 square metres. A restaurant kitchen runs 40 to 100. Same plates expected, one-tenth the room.

The chefs who thrive stopped treating that as a handicap and started treating it as a design parameter, built around three mental zones. The ones who don't spend every service fighting the room instead of cooking in it.

The three-zone system is laid out in the article. Link in bio.
posts.why: Startling space-ratio number inverts the expectation (tiny space, restaurant output) and reframes a limitation as design, withholding the system.

posts.subhook: The gap that saves your cold plates
posts.caption:
Mediterranean summer pushes ambient galley temperature to 35C. Without a 60cm gap or a thermal barrier between your hot and cold zones, your cold assembly is compromised before it ever reaches the pass.

Most galleys plate salads and desserts a hand's width from a screaming salamander and wonder why the work goes slack. The fix is a layout rule, not more ice.

The hot-cold zoning spec is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Exact temp (35C) and exact distance (60cm) name a hidden failure most chefs commit daily; stakes are concrete and the rule is withheld.

posts.subhook: Skip it and you react all day
posts.caption:
Skip the 15-minute morning reset and you spend the entire service reacting instead of executing.

Five moves, before a single burner goes on: inventory sweep, temperature log, equipment check, service brief, prep prioritisation. It's the difference between a galley that runs to your intentions and one that ambushes you at 9pm when the 7pm service slips on a weather delay.

The full Daily Reset Protocol is in the article. Link in bio.
posts.why: A firm time box (15 min) plus a vivid stakes line (react vs execute, 7pm becomes 9pm) and the five steps teased but not detailed.

posts.subhook: On a rolling boat, motion is danger
posts.caption:
In a confined galley, an unnecessary turn isn't inefficiency. It's a hot pan, a sharp knife, and a rolling deck that doesn't forgive you.

The rule is brutally simple: work flows one direction, ingredients in, plates out. Dominant hand faces the heat. Garbage positioned for a drop, not a throw. Break the flow and the room starts cooking you back.

The full motion-economy breakdown is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Reframes efficiency as physical danger (contrarian stakes) with concrete one-direction rules, leaving the full method as the loop to close.

sea-urchin-seasonal-guide.title: Sea Urchin: Seasonal Guide, Compound Profile, and Sourcing

posts.caption:
Right now Paracentrotus lividus is at pre-spawning maximum. Protein, fat, carotenoids, free glutamate — all at their annual ceiling. In about six weeks spawning begins and every one of those numbers falls off a cliff.

The mistake: treating urchin as a year-round luxury when it's really a January–February peak that pays you back for exactly one window. Buy it in March and you're paying premium money for a depleted gonad.

The exact metrics — and how to read a roe for peak — are in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Conflict + ticking-clock number (six weeks) creates urgency; withholds the how/why.

posts.caption:
Up to 40% of an urchin's total fatty acids are EPA and DHA at pre-spawning peak — the same omega-3s in salmon and mackerel. That's not trivia. That fat fraction IS the melting, creamy texture you're paying for on the plate.

Which means the season you buy in decides the mouthfeel you serve. Off-peak roe doesn't just taste flatter — the physics of the cream is gone.

The full compound profile — fat, protein, pigment — is in the article. Link in bio.
posts.why: Startling number tied directly to a texture the chef can taste — specific payoff.

posts.caption:
An urchin's flavour is a direct chemical transcript of the rock it grazed. Move it 50 kilometres down the coast and the flavour shifts — because the encrusting algae it eats changed. This is terroir in its most literal, edible form.

So "sea urchin" on an invoice tells you almost nothing. The coastline does. Source blind and you can't explain to a guest why one box sings and the next is flat.

What actually drives the flavour — and the sourcing spec that protects it — is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Contrarian reframe (same species, different food) + concrete 50km number opens a sourcing curiosity gap.

posts.caption:
The deep orange of urchin roe is echinenone — a keto-carotenoid that makes up 50–60% of the gonad's pigment. The same family of compound that turns flamingo feathers pink and colours cyanobacteria.

This matters at the pass: pale, washed-out lobes aren't a lighting problem, they're a pigment-depletion signal. Colour is a freshness and peak-season readout you can use with your eyes before you ever taste.

How to read colour, firmness and holding time is in the article. Link in bio.
posts.why: Surprising cross-domain fact (flamingo) hooks, then converts to a usable at-the-pass quality signal.

posts.caption:
Whole urchins hold 72 hours refrigerated — but only cup-side down on damp seaweed. Shuck them and the clock drops to 24. Freeze them and you've ruined them. Full stop.

Most galley waste with urchin isn't the buy, it's the hold: stored upside down, on the wrong bed, shucked too early on a charter that slipped a day. One wrong call and a premium box is bin.

The complete sourcing and storage spec — what to look for, how to keep it — is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Three hard numbers + a costly mistake (waste) make the stakes immediate and personal.

sea-urchin-beurre-blanc.title: Sea Urchin Beurre Blanc: Roe as Natural Emulsifier

posts.caption:
Sea urchin roe stabilises a beurre blanc with no cream, no starch, no egg. The roe is ~20% lipid, and its phospholipid lipoproteins lock the oil-water interface the exact same way an egg yolk does in hollandaise. So you get the structure of an emulsifier and a hit of free glutamic acid that reads as deep umami, not fish.

Most chefs reach for cream to insure a beurre blanc. This does the opposite: it removes the safety net and ends up more stable, with a coral colour cream could never give you. The catch is one number that decides whether it's silk or scrambled.

Full mechanism and the four-step build are in the piece — link in bio.
posts.why: Contrarian claim that overturns a foundational sauce assumption (egg/cream as the only emulsifiers), backed by a specific mechanism.

posts.subhook: The line between silk and grit
posts.caption:
Above 65°C the roe proteins tighten and your sauce turns grainy in seconds. That's the single failure point in a sea urchin beurre blanc, and almost everyone hits it by blending while the pan is still hot.

The fix is uncomfortable for line cooks: pull it off the heat, let it fall to 58°C, then add the roe. Too cautious and it won't integrate. Too eager and it breaks. There's a tight window and a hard ceiling, and the article gives you both numbers plus the 10-second rule that keeps it from thinning out.

The full temperature map is in the piece — link in bio.
posts.why: A startling specific number as the whole hook; names the exact mistake (blending hot) and the stakes (grainy broken sauce).

posts.caption:
Ten seconds with the immersion blender. Go longer and you whip air into the emulsion and it thins out on the plate — a failure that looks like success right up until it slumps.

The sea urchin beurre blanc is a sauce of hard limits: 10 seconds max, 50g roe to 200g butter, off heat below 65°C. Hit all three and it coats a spoon and holds 15 to 20 minutes at 55°C in a bain-marie without breaking. Miss one and you start over.

The exact ratios and the four-step build are in the piece — link in bio.
posts.why: Counterintuitive (more blending = worse), one precise number, and the hidden-failure framing raises the stakes.

posts.subhook: Why uni reads savoury, not seafood
posts.caption:
Free glutamic acid is why sea urchin deepens a sauce instead of making it taste fishy. Blended into warm butter, the roe amplifies savoury depth the way good stock does — it just happens to also be doing the structural work of an egg yolk at the same time.

That's the trick worth stealing even if you never serve uni again: an ingredient that emulsifies AND seasons in one move. ~15% protein, ~20% lipid at pre-spawning peak, all of it earning its place on the plate. But it only behaves if the roe is fresh, firm, deep orange — frozen breaks it every time.

The sourcing rules and the full why-it-works are in the piece — link in bio.
posts.why: Myth-bust on a common fear (uni = fishy), with the specific compound named and a sourcing stakes-twist at the end.

silver-lining-protocol.title: The Silver Lining Protocol: A Science-Backed Survival Guide for Galley Sanity

posts.subhook: Low mood is the season, not you
posts.caption:
82% of superyacht crew report low mood or anxiety onboard. The biggest barrier to getting help isn't stigma. It's time. Crew cannot stop.

So this isn't a pep talk. It's a clinical protocol built from submarines, Antarctic winter-overs, and NASA missions. Nothing in it takes more than five minutes. Most take under sixty seconds, doable standing at the stove.

The full survival guide for galley sanity is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Startling number as the cold open, then a credibility pivot (submarines/NASA) plus the real barrier nobody names (time, not stigma) to widen the curiosity gap.

posts.caption:
2 seconds in, 8 seconds out. That exact ratio is where your heart rate variability actually shifts and cortisol drops, according to a meta-analysis of 223 breathing studies. Most people exhale too fast and get nothing.

The long exhale is what stimulates the vagus nerve and overrides your stress response. Even 3 to 4 cycles produce a measurable change, between courses, at the stove, before sleep.

The full reset, plus the wrist trick that does the same thing in 30 seconds, is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: A precise, testable number (2:8) plus the named mistake (exhaling too fast = nothing) makes it impossible not to want the mechanism.

posts.subhook: Be specific or it does nothing
posts.caption:
"I'm grateful for crew" does almost nothing. "The second stew covered my section without being asked" rewires your appraisal circuits. Specificity is the entire mechanism, and generic gratitude is background noise your brain ignores.

The payoff isn't soft: a meta-analysis of 64 trials links grateful cognition to 23% lower cortisol. Three specific lines before sleep, four nights a week, measurably lowered cortisol in both waking and sleep.

The exact 2-minute protocol is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Contrarian myth-bust (the gratitude everyone does is useless) plus a hard number (23%) reframes a soft topic as a measurable intervention.

posts.caption:
Run cold water over your inner wrists for 30 seconds. The radial and ulnar arteries sit right at the surface there, so the cold stimulates the vagus nerve and triggers an immediate calming response. No app, no break room, no one even notices.

It's one of three galley-compatible micro-breaks in the piece, and the research is blunt: breaks under 10 minutes raise vigor and cut fatigue, and people who take them score 22% higher on workplace resilience.

The other two resets are in the article. Link in bio.
posts.why: An oddly specific physical hack (cold water, inner wrists, 30s) is instantly try-able and screams curiosity; the 22% stat closes the value.

posts.subhook: The reframe with the biggest effect
posts.caption:
Same confined boat, same brutal hours, two outcomes. Astronauts and submariners who frame confinement as a mission with a purpose and a defined end report significantly better psychological outcomes than those who feel it's being done to them.

NASA built a whole protocol around it. The line that flips it: you chose this, you're on an expedition, it ends on a specific date. That single reframe is rated the most powerful element of the framework.

The full NASA CONNECT framework, translated for the galley, is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: A binary either/or hook (prison vs expedition) creates instant tension, and the NASA pedigree plus 'most powerful element' framing pulls for the how.

medicinal-plants-your-galley.title: The Pharmacy in Your Spice Rack: What Science Actually Knows About Medicinal Plants in Food

posts.caption: Crush your garlic, then walk away from the stove for ten minutes. That pause is the whole point. Crush a clove and the enzyme alliinase starts converting alliin into allicin, the compound that does the work. Throw it straight into hot oil and you kill the enzyme before it finishes, you get flavour but almost none of the bioactive payload. Ten minutes of patience measurably increases the active compounds. Most cooks crush and toss it in within seconds. The full chemistry, and nine other plants you're underusing, is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Counterintuitive technique with a specific number and a clear mistake the reader is making tonight

posts.caption: A turmeric latte with no black pepper is, in the article's word, marketing. Your gut absorbs roughly 1% of curcumin on its own. Add black pepper and absorption jumps about 2,000%. Add fat on top and you hit optimal. Indian kitchens solved this 4,000 years ago, every curry is turmeric plus pepper plus fat, and they had no idea why it worked. Now we do. If you're plating golden milk or turmeric eggs without the pepper, you're serving colour, not medicine. The absorption table and the mechanism are in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Myth-bust on a trendy ingredient plus a startling 2,000% number and the fix

posts.caption: The peppery burn at the back of your throat from good olive oil is a drug. It's oleocanthal, identified in 2005 when a researcher noticed fresh EVOO stung exactly like liquid ibuprofen, then confirmed it blocks the same COX enzymes. About 50ml of premium oil delivers roughly 10% of an adult ibuprofen dose. The stronger the sting, the higher the concentration, which means a smooth, tasteless oil isn't just boring, it's less protective. The PREDIMED trial tied 40 to 60ml a day to a 30% drop in cardiovascular events. That's a drug-level effect from a finishing oil. The full story is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Surprising fact reframing a daily ingredient as a drug, with two hard numbers

posts.subhook: Six trials. One winner you didn't expect.
posts.caption: 30mg of saffron a day matched Prozac for mild-to-moderate depression across six clinical trials, with fewer side effects. Its compounds, crocin and safranal, cross the blood-brain barrier and modulate serotonin. Persians have cooked saffron into rice and stews for millennia and consume more of it per capita than any nation on earth. You already keep it in the galley for risotto and seafood. You probably didn't know what a pinch was doing. The mechanism, the dose, and four other galley spices with confirmed pharmacology are in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Bold head-to-head claim against a known drug, backed by trial count and dose

posts.subhook: And you're dosing a liver toxin
posts.caption: Supermarket cinnamon is almost always Cassia, and Cassia carries coumarin, a compound that's hepatotoxic at high doses. Ceylon cinnamon has about 250 times less of it. So the spice you're shaking into porridge and grain salads every day could be the wrong one entirely. And it matters more than flavour, because cinnamaldehyde mimics insulin at the cellular level, with trials showing 1 to 6g daily cutting fasting blood glucose by 10 to 29% in type 2 diabetics. Ceylon tastes better anyway, more complex, less aggressive. How to tell them apart, and the therapeutic dose, is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Stakes-driven warning that the reader's pantry choice is wrong, plus a 250x contrast

umami-western-cuisine.title: Umami in Western Cuisine: Fermentation as the Universal Glutamate Engine

posts.caption: Marmite carries more free glutamate than 24-month Parmigiano-Reggiano: 1,960 mg/100g against roughly 1,680. The savory depth you think is uniquely Asian or uniquely Italian is just fermentation generating glutamate, and the West has done it for centuries. Most chefs reach for soy or miso by reflex and never touch the jar that out-scores both. The full ranking of Western umami sources is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Startling specific number plus contrarian ranking: the least glamorous pantry item beats the prestige one.

posts.caption: Out of miso in a remote Caribbean port and the menu still needs depth. Stack three Western glutamate sources instead: Parmesan rind in the stock, anchovy paste working behind the scenes, Worcestershire as a finishing agent. That is a backup umami architecture built entirely from a European pantry, no Asian ingredients required. Mouritsen calls the deliberate version umamification. The exact roles each source plays are in the article. Link in bio.
posts.why: Solves a concrete provisioning nightmare specific to the audience; withholds the how.

posts.caption: Western and Asian umami sources share the exact same glutamate backbone, but they are NOT interchangeable on the plate. Same engine, different volatile superstructure, so anchovy where the dish wanted miso reads wrong even when the savoriness lands. The pros use both traditions and substitute neither. The distinction most cooks miss is spelled out in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Myth-bust that corrects a common shortcut chefs feel clever for using.

posts.subhook: The rind you keep throwing out
posts.caption: That Parmigiano rind in the bin is carrying 1,200 to 1,680 mg of free glutamate per 100g, built up by proteolysis over 24 months of aging. Drop it in the stock and you are stacking aged-cheese umami for free, no Asian pantry item involved. Most galleys compost the single highest-value flavour source they already own. The full table of Western glutamate sources, ranked, is in the article. Link in bio.
posts.why: Names a specific waste habit with a precise number; reframes trash as the best ingredient.

posts.caption: Worcestershire sauce delivers 830 mg/100g of free glutamate, and it gets there through fermented anchovy plus malt-vinegar aging, the same mechanism as a fish sauce most chefs would never use so casually. It is one of six Western condiments that build umami the centuries-old way, no glutamate powder required. Where each one ranks, and how to deploy them, is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Surprising reframe of a familiar bottle plus a concrete number opens a curiosity gap.

spaghetti-ricci-di-mare.title: Spaghetti ai Ricci di Mare: Mondello, Sicily

posts.caption:
Above 65°C, sea urchin roe seizes: grainy, bitter, ruined. The whole dish lives or dies in a 10-degree window the proteins will not forgive.

Most cooks fold the roe into a hot pan and wonder why it turned to bitter paste. The Sicilian grandmothers hit ~50-55°C by instinct — warm enough to release the aroma, never enough to tighten the protein. We pulled the denaturation numbers and they were right to the degree.

The exact window, and the off-heat move that protects it, is in the piece — link in bio.
posts.why: Startling specific number (65°C) + the failure mode they're probably causing = personal stakes, curiosity gap on the safe window.

posts.caption:
No Parmesan. Ever. On the most famous urchin pasta in Sicily, cheese is a mistake — and the reason is chemistry, not tradition.

Parmesan's glutamate is so loud it buries the roe's quieter umami. Cream masks it. Butter competes. The classic uses four ingredients precisely because anything richer drowns the one flavour you paid for.

The full short list — and why each addition kills the dish — is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Contrarian myth-bust (no cheese on pasta) with a chemical reason — challenges instinct, opens a gap on what else is banned.

posts.caption:
This dish exists because sea urchins were free. No boat, no net, no bait — P. lividus clings to every rock in the intertidal zone, harvestable with bare hands and cold water.

For Sicilian fishing families where protein was seasonal and poverty was structural, urchins were the one food that cost nothing but your hands. Austerity wasn't a style choice. It was the whole point — and it's why the recipe refuses cream, butter, and cheese to this day.

The origin and the four-ingredient method are in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Surprising origin-story hook (poverty, not luxury) reframes a delicacy — narrative curiosity, ideal for a slow weekend read.

posts.caption:
Shucked urchin roe loses its flavour by the clock: oxidation degrades the glutamic acid within 24 hours. The umami you're plating tomorrow is already gone.

This is why the classic is non-negotiable about same-day roe — and why a Lipari morning market, urchins harvested that dawn, beats anything sitting on ice. Twelve minutes from boiling water to plate, by design.

The sourcing-to-service timeline is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Concrete decay number (24h) attacks the assumption that roe keeps — sourcing stakes for chefs who pre-prep.

posts.caption:
Twelve minutes from boiling water to plate — that's the entire dish. Anchor off the Aeolians, urchins from the dawn market at Lipari, served at lunch the same day they were pulled.

Four ingredients, one coastline, no shortcuts. The skill isn't complexity — it's the off-heat timing and a 10-degree temperature window most cooks blow straight through.

The full method, plus the Etna Carricante to pour beside it, is in the piece. Link in bio.
posts.why: Tight specific number (12 min) sells effortlessness, then reveals the hidden difficulty — hooks chefs on a serveable, charter-ready dish.

myojaku-three-stars.title: Myojaku Earns Three Stars and STCW Anti-Harassment Training Goes Mandatory

posts.caption:
A Tokyo chef just earned three Michelin stars by deleting the one thing Japanese cooking treats as non-negotiable: dashi.

While the rest of us stack glutamate sources to build depth, Hidetoshi Nakamura did the opposite. He stripped the stock back and forced every single ingredient to carry its own umami. Myojaku opened in 2022, took its first star in 2023, and hit the top tier in under four years. The only new three-star in the entire 2026 Tokyo guide.

If your instinct is always to add more, this is the case for the other direction. We break down exactly what he replaced the dashi with.

The full piece is on the site. Link in bio.
posts.why: Contrarian myth-bust + a startling number (under 4 years, only new 3-star). Withholds what replaced the dashi.

posts.caption:
Two opposite philosophies. Same verdict from the inspectors.

One path: amplify. Layer your glutamate sources, build umami like a wall. The other: reveal. Pull the dashi out entirely and let the ingredient stand alone. Michelin just confirmed both lead to three stars in the exact same Tokyo guide.

Most chefs only ever practise one of these. The mistake is thinking depth has a single direction. We lay out when amplification wins and when revelation does, and why the choice is about the ingredient in front of you, not the technique you trust.

Full breakdown on the site. Link in bio.
posts.why: Frames a clean either/or (amplify vs reveal) the chef can apply tonight; curiosity gap on which to use when.

posts.caption:
Michelin's oldest active recognised chef is 97. He has cooked eel the same way, in the same place, for longer than most of us have been alive.

Kenjiro Kanemoto took the 2026 Mentor Chef Award in the same guide that crowned a radical minimalist with three stars. One man rebuilt the rules. The other never changed a thing. Both honoured in the same breath.

The lesson for anyone chasing the next technique is uncomfortable, and worth sitting with. We make the case for mastery over novelty.

Read it on the site. Link in bio.
posts.why: Striking number (97, 70 years, one dish) + the tension of tradition vs novelty. Emotional, scroll-stopping.

posts.caption:
From January 1, 2026, STCW Basic Safety Training carries mandatory modules on harassment, bullying and violence at sea. The "continuum of harm" is now codified in maritime training for the first time.

Here is the part most sole chefs miss: if you renewed your STCW before 2026, you are not required to retake it, so nobody will tell you this happened. MCA, Marshall Islands and Cayman are already confirmed. Your vessel's SMS is supposed to be updated now, not next refit.

We spell out exactly what changed, who it covers, and the one question to put to your captain this week.

Full detail on the site. Link in bio.
posts.why: Personal stakes + a gap (pre-2026 renewers won't be told). Specific flag states and effective date make it credible and urgent.

posts.caption:
If your STCW renewal predates 2026, you slipped through a gap. The new mandatory anti-harassment modules are not retroactive, so no one is obligated to flag them to you.

That is the trap for a sole chef. The training, the bystander responsibilities, the codified "continuum of harm", all live with crew who certify from January onward. Your management company can run a refresher. The MCA strongly recommends it. But the move starts with you raising it.

We lay out the exact ask to bring to your captain, and what your vessel's policies should already say.

Read it on the site. Link in bio.
posts.why: Direct second-person callout to the at-risk reader (pre-2026 renewers); makes the omission feel personal and actionable.
